title: Source lineage for The Four Sons of Aymon (aha2639)
summary: The repository XML is a local representation of University of Michigan CME item AHA2639, encoded in 2006 from Octavia Richardson's two-part EETS edition. Part I was issued as Extra Series 44 in 1884 with title-page evidence dated 1885; Part II was issued as Extra Series 45 in 1885 with XML title-page evidence dated 1886. Richardson's edited Middle English text is composite: the surviving Caxton copy now Manchester 19180 supplies the base from Richardson page 24 onward, while British Library C.12.i.7, William Copland's 1554 print, supplies four bounded prose runs (the opening and three internal losses), two chapter headings, and a four-word repair at page 591. Richardson collated against British Library IB.42244, now attributed to the Printer of L'Abusé en court edition, but neither that item nor the competing Guillaume Le Roy edition is identified as Caxton's exemplar. Complete local cache coverage includes the 710-page Richardson scan and OCR, all 550 canvases of the distinct Manchester facsimile, and complete 456- and 465-canvas Gallica bundles for exact BnF copies of the two French candidate print states; those French bundles contain the source work but not the cataloged English target.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for aha2639
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The file is 1,279,875 bytes and uses the DLPSTEXTCLASS family.
Its exact transfer path from U-M to the CME submodule is not documented.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AHA2639
entities.description: A keyed and structurally marked-up electronic representation of Richardson's two-part edition, including Richardson's introduction and story sketch and the edited work through page 592, but excluding the glossarial and name indexes on pages 593-667.
notes: The header declares generic eng even though the cataloged work is Middle English.
The encoding contains 1,585 NOTE1 elements overall; 1,550 occur in the target work, principally Richardson's French comparison apparatus.

entities.label: Richardson's Four Sons of Aymon, Part I, EETS Extra Series 44
entities.description: The first physical issue of Richardson's composite scholarly edition. It contains title and front matter, the introduction and story sketch, and the edited work through page 320, breaking within Chapter XIV.

date_statements.label: title-page date
date_statements.notes: The title-page date is not silently substituted for the EETS issue year.

bibliographic.citation: William Caxton, The Right Plesaunt and Goodly Historie of the Foure Sonnes of Aymon, ed. Octavia Richardson, English Charlemagne Romances Part X, EETS Extra Series 44 (London: N. Trübner for EETS, issued 1884; title page 1885), Part I.
bibliographic.title: The Right Plesaunt and Goodly Historie of the Foure Sonnes of Aymon, Part I
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 44; English Charlemagne Romances, Part X
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

notes: The target work begins on printed page 3; pages v-xxix are Richardson's introduction and story sketch.
The issue ends at page 320 within Chapter XIV; page 321 begins Part II and changes the running footer from CHARL. ROM. X to CHARL. ROM. XI.

entities.label: Richardson's Four Sons of Aymon, Part II, EETS Extra Series 45
entities.description: The second physical issue of Richardson's composite scholarly edition. It continues Chapter XIV on page 321, ends the cataloged work on page 592, and adds a glossarial index on pages 593-624 and a name index on pages 625-667.

date_statements.label: title-page date represented by the XML
date_statements.notes: The XML concatenates the two Roman-numeral title-page dates. The cached combined scan does not preserve a separately inspectable Part II title leaf.

bibliographic.citation: William Caxton, The Right Plesaunt and Goodly Historie of the Foure Sonnes of Aymon, ed. Octavia Richardson, English Charlemagne Romances Part XI, EETS Extra Series 45 (London: N. Trübner for EETS, issued 1885; XML title-page evidence 1886), Part II.
bibliographic.title: The Right Plesaunt and Goodly Historie of the Foure Sonnes of Aymon, Part II
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 45; English Charlemagne Romances, Part XI
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society

notes: The target work occupies pages 321-592; the U-M encoding excludes the glossarial and name indexes on pages 593-667.

entities.label: Internet Archive combined scan of Richardson EETS 44-45
entities.description: A 710-page scan from the University of Toronto copy, preserving Richardson's complete combined edition, including the target work, front matter, glossarial index, and name index.
entities.reproduction_format: 710-page PDF, full OCR text, page-number metadata, and Internet Archive item metadata

entities.label: William Caxton's Westminster print of The Four Sons of Aymon, about 1490
entities.description: An abstract print-state node for Caxton's first Middle English edition. It connects the surviving Manchester copy and Cambridge fragment without treating either one's current losses as the intended extent of the work.

bibliographic.citation: The Four Sons of Aymon, trans. William Caxton (Westminster: William Caxton, about 1490), ISTC ia01434500.
bibliographic.title: The Four Sons of Aymon

notes: The print has no surviving title page, printer statement, place, date, or colophon in the Manchester copy; modern attribution and dating depend on bibliographical evidence.
Commissioned by John, Earl of Oxford according to Caxton's prologue and current catalogs.

entities.label: University of Manchester Library reference 19180, Caxton's Four Sons of Aymon
entities.description: The principal surviving bound copy of Caxton's c. 1490 print and Richardson's base witness. It begins at signature B.iii.a within Chapter I, has three material internal losses and a small late cut-out, ends with AMEN, and lacks the colophon.

holding.notes: Early Printing Collection; former owners include John Ratcliffe, James West, and George John Spencer, Earl Spencer.

notes: The current IIIF object has 550 canvases including bindings, endpapers, blanks, and every imaged leaf side.
Visual inspection places the surviving Caxton text on canvases 11-544; canvases 1-10 and 545-550 are binding or end matter, and canvases 77-80 are blank surfaces at a documented internal loss.

entities.label: University of Manchester IIIF facsimile of reference 19180
entities.description: The distinct 550-canvas LUNA/IIIF digital presentation of the present Manchester reference 19180 bound object. It reproduces the physical witness binding-to-binding but does not repair that witness's textual losses.
entities.reproduction_format: 550-canvas color IIIF Presentation object with Image API services
notes: Surviving target text occupies canvases 11-544; complete digital coverage of the current physical object is distinct from textual completeness.
The exact item record applies CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 to the images, while the IIIF manifest's license field is null.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library Inc.3.J.1.1[3522], two-leaf Caxton fragment
entities.description: A separately held fragment of Caxton's print, now consisting of two leaves from gathering E. The current Cambridge record says there were formerly four and that the other two have been missing since at least 1981.

historical_designations: formerly four leaves from gathering E
holding.notes: From Henry Bradshaw's library and presented by his friends in 1887; mounted in a folder.

notes: ISTC still reports four leaves as of the current record checked 2026-07-16; the current holding statement is retained separately rather than overwritten.

entities.label: British Library C.12.i.7, William Copland's 1554 Four Sons of Aymon
entities.description: The exact physical copy Richardson used to supply missing Caxton text, two chapter headings, and a small repair. The British Library current record identifies the Thomas Petet issue printed by William Copland in 1554.

bibliographic.citation: The Right Plesaunt and Goodly Historie of the Foure Sonnes of Aymon (London: William Copland for Thomas Petet, 6 May 1554), British Library C.12.i.7.
bibliographic.title: The Right Plesaunt and Goodly Historie of the Foure Sonnes of Aymon

holding.notes: Current catalog status available at St Pancras when checked 2026-07-16.

notes: Richardson's exact source is this BL item, not the Morgan or Folger copies of other 1554 trade issues.

entities.label: Anonymous fifteenth-century French prose Les quatre fils Aymon
entities.description: An abstract work-level node for the anonymous French prose adaptation of the Renaut de Montauban tradition. It connects distinct printed versions and Caxton's unidentified exemplar without asserting that any surviving item was his copy.
notes: The older verse chanson Renaut de Montauban is a mediated ancestor, not Caxton's direct source.

entities.label: Unidentified physical French exemplar translated by William Caxton
entities.description: The French prose book that John, Earl of Oxford sent to Caxton and that Caxton says he translated and printed. Its edition, copy identity, provenance, and present location are not established.
notes: Missing means not presently identified, not proven destroyed.
Hellinga identifies the Guillaume Le Roy and Printer of L'Abusé en court Lyon editions as alternatives but says the exact edition cannot be decided without extensive textual investigation.
Richardson's use of BL IB.42244 for collation is not item-level evidence that Caxton used that same copy.

entities.label: Guillaume Le Roy Lyon edition of Les quatre fils Aymon, about 1482-1486
entities.description: An abstract early Lyon edition/state and one of the two print classes Hellinga considers a possible source for Caxton. It remains distinct from the exact BnF RES-Y2-365 physical copy and that copy's Gallica facsimile.

bibliographic.citation: Les quatre fils Aymon ([Lyon]: Guillaume Le Roy, about 1482-85; CIBN about 1485-86), ISTC ia01432800.
bibliographic.title: Les quatre fils Aymon

notes: A candidate edition class only; no relation identifies this state or any surviving copy as Caxton's exemplar.

entities.label: BnF RES-Y2-365, Guillaume Le Roy Les quatre fils Aymon
entities.description: The exact BnF Réserve des livres rares physical copy assigned to the Guillaume Le Roy edition/state. It contains the French source work and not the cataloged English target.

holding.notes: BnF, département Réserve des livres rares.

notes: This physical supporting witness is not identified as Caxton's exemplar.

entities.label: Gallica IIIF facsimile of BnF RES-Y2-365
entities.description: The distinct 456-canvas Gallica digital presentation of the exact BnF RES-Y2-365 physical copy.
entities.reproduction_format: 456-canvas Gallica IIIF Presentation object with Image API services
notes: The complete private cache retains all 456 published canvases at a practical 1600-pixel-width profile with native fallback for narrow surfaces.
The French source work is present; the cataloged English target work is absent and no work_portion is asserted.

entities.label: Printer of L'Abusé en court Lyon edition of Les quatre fils Aymon, about 1483-1485
entities.description: An abstract early Lyon edition/state and one of the two print classes Hellinga considers a possible source for Caxton. It remains distinct from both the exact BnF RES-Y2-364 physical copy and BL IB.42244, Richardson's comparison witness.

bibliographic.citation: Les quatre fils Aymon ([Lyon]: Printer of L'Abusé en court, about 1485; CIBN about 1483-85), ISTC ia01432900.
bibliographic.title: Les quatre fils Aymon

notes: A candidate edition class only; Richardson's comparison against BL IB.42244 does not identify Caxton's physical exemplar.

entities.label: BnF RES-Y2-364, Printer of L'Abusé en court Les quatre fils Aymon
entities.description: The exact BnF Réserve des livres rares physical copy assigned to the Printer of L'Abusé en court edition/state. It is distinct from BL IB.42244 and contains the French source work, not the cataloged English target.

holding.notes: BnF, département Réserve des livres rares; this item is not BL IB.42244.

notes: This physical supporting witness is not identified as Caxton's exemplar.

entities.label: Gallica IIIF facsimile of BnF RES-Y2-364
entities.description: The distinct 465-canvas Gallica digital presentation of the exact BnF RES-Y2-364 physical copy.
entities.reproduction_format: 465-canvas Gallica IIIF Presentation object with Image API services
notes: The complete private cache retains all 465 published canvases at a practical 1600-pixel-width profile with native fallback for narrow surfaces.
The French source work is present; the cataloged English target work is absent and no work_portion is asserted.

entities.label: British Library IB.42244, Richardson's French comparison copy
entities.description: The exact British Museum volume Richardson calls a Lyon edition of about 1480 and cites throughout as F. orig. Current cataloging retains the older 1480? date, while ISTC assigns the item to the Printer of L'Abusé en court edition about 1485.

date_statements.label: current ISTC print attribution and date
date_statements.value: Printer of L'Abusé en court, about 1485; CIBN about 1483-85

holding.notes: Current catalog status available at St Pancras when checked 2026-07-16.

physical_description.extent: 228 leaves; first and last leaves wanting

notes: Identification as Richardson's comparison item is supported by the unique combination of repository, historical Lyon 1480? cataloging, folio format, 228-leaf extent, missing boundary leaves, and matching opening and explicit.
This editorial comparison role is distinct from the unresolved question of Caxton's exemplar.

entities.label: Early English Text Society List of Publications 2026
entities.description: The current official EETS publication list used to verify separate issue years, series numbers, ISBNs, prices, and availability for Richardson Parts I and II.

entities.label: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright (April 2026)
entities.description: The April 2026 official duration guide used only for the United States rights assessment of historical publications; it is supporting legal evidence and not part of the work's textual lineage.

scope.notes: The later title-page reproduction is editorial carrier matter; the scoped target text starts with Caxton's prologue on p. 3.

subject.description: One-sheet loss within Chapter II supplied from Copland
locators: after 'entred in to ye grete forest of Ardeyne' through 'the whiche dyd so without delaye'

scope.notes: Richardson's note says one sheet is lost and marks Caxton's return before the Chapter III heading.

subject.description: Two-sheet loss within Chapter III supplied from Copland
locators: p. 92 line 18 after 'After souper' through p. 96 line 20
ends immediately before 'and the XII other'

scope.notes: Richardson marks the start after 'After souper' and the return of Caxton on p. 96.

subject.description: Two-page loss within Chapter VIII supplied from Copland
locators: p. 186 line 22 within 'Rycharde of Normandy' through p. 188 line 28
ends immediately before 'not that my folke have ony dommage'
between Caxton signatures L.viii and M.i verso as marked by Richardson

scope.notes: Richardson marks a two-page Caxton loss and the continuation from Caxton on p. 188.

subject.description: Two surviving leaves from gathering E

subject.description: Copland's later English printed edition

scope.notes: Richardson calls Copland's print the third edition and treats its prologue as a literal reprint; this is a textual-edition relation, not physical descent from the Manchester copy.

subject.description: Caxton's translated twenty-eight-chapter romance narrative

qpdf 12.3.2 reports no syntax or stream-encoding errors; pdfinfo 26.07.0 reads all 710 pages; pdftotext 26.07.0 extracts the complete document without error.

qpdf 12.3.2 reports recoverable linearization hint-table warnings (object order, offsets, counts, and shared identifiers) and says the operation succeeded with warnings. pdfinfo 26.07.0 reads all 40 pages, and pdftotext 26.07.0 extracts the complete document without error.
The official provider bytes are retained unchanged; no repair or relinearization was performed.

local_copies.label: Complete Manchester reference 19180 IIIF bundle at 1600-pixel width

work_portion.label: Surviving Caxton text of The Four Sons of Aymon
locators: canvas 11 begins at signature B.iii.a, Richardson p. 24 line 12
canvases 1-10 and 545-550 are binding/end matter outside the text
notes: The complete provider object is locally present, but the physical witness is textually imperfect and includes blank surfaces at internal losses.
The ZIP preserves the IIIF manifest, exact request inventory, checksums, and all 550 requested JPEG derivatives at effective 1600-pixel width.
Archive audit on 2026-07-16: CRC clean; all 550 inventoried member byte counts and SHA-256 hashes match; the manifest has 550 canvases; no unexpected members.
The item metadata applies CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 to images; the IIIF manifest's license field is null. Both facts are preserved in rights records.

Supporting French candidate source only: the cataloged English target work is absent; no work_portion is asserted. Source gallica.bnf.fr / Bibliothèque nationale de France.

access.format: 456-canvas Gallica IIIF object; complete 1600-pixel-width bundle, original manifest, and catalog metadata cached
notes: This is a supporting candidate source, not an identified Caxton exemplar; no target English work portion is claimed.
Gallica permits attributed noncommercial reuse of this BnF public-domain reproduction, while commercial reuse requires its paid-license route; the cache remains private and gitignored.

rights.basis: The current exact U-M item page labels the materials Public Domain and applies the DPLA No Copyright-United States statement.
notes: No Copyright-United States is jurisdiction-specific and is not a worldwide dedication.
The legacy availability statement embedded in the 2006 XML remains a separate, less specific provider assertion.

rights.component: Legacy availability statement embedded in the 2006 XML
rights.basis: The XML says that copyright applies where applicable and that other distribution restrictions may apply, without naming a license or resolving the exact restriction.
notes: This record preserves the historical wording without assuming that it overrides U-M's current item-level Public Domain / No Copyright-United States presentation.

rights.component: Pinned CME XML bytes, textual transcription, and structural markup
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository carries the Unlicense and describes software, but the reviewed notice does not expressly distinguish or assign the historical text, modern transcription, and XML markup layers.
notes: Do not infer that a software-focused Unlicense notice conclusively resolves rights in third-party textual or editorial content.
The General Corpus repository's own GPL and CC BY-SA statements address code and output separately and do not remove this source-layer uncertainty.

rights.component: Caxton's about-1490 Middle English text and historical print content
rights.basis: The English work was printed about 1490 and is far outside any United States copyright term.
notes: This does not grant rights in modern photographs, catalog prose, digital interfaces, or later editorial apparatus.

rights.component: Copland's 1554 English text and historical print content
rights.basis: The edition was printed in 1554 and is far outside any United States copyright term.
notes: This conclusion concerns historical text and print content, not British Library catalog prose or any modern reproduction.

rights.component: Richardson's Part I edition, introduction, transcription, collation, and apparatus
rights.basis: Part I was issued in 1884, with a preserved title page dated 1885, and is well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.

rights.component: Richardson's Part II edition, transcription, collation, indexes, and apparatus
rights.basis: Part II was issued in 1885, while the XML's composite title-page transcription supplies an 1886 carrier date; either publication date is well before the current United States public-domain cutoff.
notes: The cached combined scan does not preserve a separately inspectable Part II title leaf, so the 1886 title-page date remains carrier evidence rather than a visually confirmed feature of that scan.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and derivative OCR/metadata
rights.basis: The exact Internet Archive metadata identifies the public download but contains no rights or license field for the scan and derivative files.
notes: The public-domain status of Richardson's underlying historical editions does not by itself determine every provider term or separate interest in the scan files.

rights.component: University of Manchester Library digital photographs and IIIF derivatives
rights.basis: The exact item metadata identifies The University of Manchester Library as image rights holder and applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0; the Library's digital-collections support page repeats that license.
notes: The IIIF Presentation manifest itself has a null license field; the exact item record supplies the controlling item-level image statement.
The ignored local cache is for research comparison and must not be copied into a public or commercial build merely because it is present locally.

rights.component: Anonymous fifteenth-century French prose textual content
rights.basis: The anonymous prose work and its two candidate Lyon print states date from the fifteenth century and are far outside any United States copyright term.
notes: This does not determine reuse conditions for Gallica's modern digital images or BnF catalog prose.

rights.component: Gallica digital images and complete IIIF surrogate of BnF RES-Y2-365
rights.basis: The exact manifest links Gallica's conditions and identifies a BnF holding without a special partner-source regime. Current terms permit free noncommercial reuse with prescribed BnF/Gallica attribution, while commercial reuse requires a paid license; Gallica supplies its IIIF API for image retrieval.
notes: Source credit: Source gallica.bnf.fr / Bibliothèque nationale de France.
The complete local bundle is a private gitignored research copy and is not licensed for unrestricted redistribution or commercial reuse.

rights.component: Gallica digital images and complete IIIF surrogate of BnF RES-Y2-364
rights.basis: The exact manifest links Gallica's conditions and identifies a BnF holding without a special partner-source regime. Current terms permit free noncommercial reuse with prescribed BnF/Gallica attribution, while commercial reuse requires a paid license; Gallica supplies its IIIF API for image retrieval.
notes: Source credit: Source gallica.bnf.fr / Bibliothèque nationale de France.
The complete local bundle is a private gitignored research copy and is not licensed for unrestricted redistribution or commercial reuse.

rights.component: Official U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A text and PDF delivery file
rights.basis: The circular is an official United States Government work; 17 U.S.C. 105 makes United States copyright protection unavailable for such works, subject to the section's exceptions.

editorial_practices.description: Richardson prints the surviving Caxton copy but supplies four bounded prose runs from William Copland's 1554 edition and supplies the headings to Chapters XI and XIII from Copland's table. The prose substitutions are the prologue/table/Chapter I opening, one lost sheet in Chapter II, two lost sheets in Chapter III, and a two-page loss in Chapter VIII.
editorial_practices.scope: Target work pp. 3-320: Copland prose at pp. 3-24 line 11, p. 65 line 6-p. 67 line 8, p. 92 line 18-p. 96 line 20, and p. 186 line 22-p. 188 line 28; Copland table headings at pp. 275 and 307
notes: The six source switches are also modeled as separate passage-scoped relations; this summary must not be read as a whole-edition attribution to Copland.

editorial_practices.description: Richardson compares the English text against the French volume now identified as British Library IB.42244 and records French equivalents, omissions, and variants in notes.
editorial_practices.scope: Part I introduction and target text pp. 3-320
notes: IB.42244 is Richardson's comparison witness only; neither Richardson's use nor its modern ISTC assignment establishes that it was Caxton's exemplar.

editorial_practices.description: Richardson prints the surviving Caxton continuation and repairs a four-word cut-out on page 591 from Copland's 1554 edition.
editorial_practices.scope: Target work pp. 321-592, specifically the bracketed words [we three th]at on p. 591
notes: The exact four-word repair is modeled as its own passage-scoped relation.

editorial_practices.description: Richardson continues comparing the English text against the French volume now identified as British Library IB.42244 and records French equivalents, omissions, and variants in notes.
notes: The comparison relation is editorial; the exact physical French exemplar used by Caxton remains unidentified.

editorial_practices.description: The electronic item encodes the combined title matter, Richardson's introduction and story sketch, and the target work through page 592, but omits Part II's glossarial and name indexes.
editorial_practices.scope: Richardson front matter and pp. v-xxix plus target pp. 3-592; omitted back matter pp. 593-667

editorial_practices.description: The U-M Level 4 TEI-in-Libraries encoding serializes two separately issued Richardson parts into one XML document and represents the target as one DIV1 containing prologue, table, and twenty-eight chapter DIV2 divisions, with printed pages, Caxton folios, notes, and inline editorial signals encoded as milestones and elements.
notes: All fifteen verse-line elements belong to Richardson's introduction; the cataloged target work itself is prose throughout.

evidence.citation: General Corpus .gitmodules entry for the CME source submodule.
evidence.locator: submodule CME
evidence.summary: The repository pins its source corpus through the Kyvero-Press/CME Git submodule; the researched checkout is at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.

evidence.citation: AHA2639 XML source in the pinned CME submodule.
evidence.locator: document root, IDNO, HEADER, and TEXT
evidence.summary: The 1,279,875-byte source identifies itself as AHA2639 and supplies the content represented by the General Corpus repository artifact; its Git blob is c6e234b0667b0e92bcb62527bcf1a36d7b63bcc9.

evidence.citation: AHA2639 XML FILEDESC, publication statement, source description, and encoded title page.
evidence.locator: HEADER/FILEDESC, HEADER/ENCODINGDESC, and TEXT/FRONT/TITLEPAGE
evidence.summary: The header identifies U-M's 2006 encoding, the Richardson EETS 44-45 source with catalog dates 1884-85, the legacy availability statement, and Level 4 TEI-in-Libraries practice. The encoded composite title page carries MDCCCLXXXV and MDCCCLXXXVI, which must remain distinct from the separate EETS issue years.

evidence.citation: AHA2639 XML Richardson introduction, Caxton prologue and table, and twenty-eight-chapter narrative.
evidence.locator: TEXT/BODY; AHA2639:1-AHA2639:3.30
evidence.summary: The body supports the French prose ancestry, Caxton's requester and translation statements, work boundary, plot, and prose form; it also retains Richardson's source discussion and inline English/French apparatus.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of the AHA2639 repository XML.
evidence.locator: TEXT/BODY element counts and DIV1/DIV2 boundaries
evidence.summary: The XML has three DIV1 divisions: Richardson's introduction, her story sketch, and the target work. The target DIV1 has thirty DIV2 children (prologue, table, and twenty-eight chapters), 439 P and 1,550 NOTE1 elements, and no L elements; all fifteen verse-line elements occur in the introduction.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, AHA2639 item information.
evidence.locator: title, creators, publication, rights, contents, and entire-text action
evidence.summary: The exact current item page confirms the AHA2639 digital text, Caxton and Richardson associations, 1884-85 Trübner source, public full-text access, Public Domain assertion, and No Copyright-United States statement.

evidence.citation: Octavia Richardson, ed., The Right Plesaunt and Goodly Historie of the Foure Sonnes of Aymon, EETS Extra Series 44-45 (London: N. Trübner, issued 1884-1885), combined complete scan.
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. v-xvi; story sketch pp. xvii-xxix; work pp. 3-592; glossarial index pp. 593-624; name index pp. 625-667
evidence.summary: The complete scan establishes Richardson's two-part edited text, source discussion, pagination, French comparison, exact Caxton/Copland switches, the preserved Part I title page dated 1885, and the work/back-matter boundary. Page 321 begins the Part XI continuation but no separate Part II title leaf is visible.

evidence.citation: Richardson's source-change statements and bracketed repair in the EETS 44-45 edition.
evidence.summary: Richardson explicitly supplies four prose runs from BL C.12.i.7 (opening through p. 24 line 11; p. 65 line 6-p. 67 line 8; p. 92 line 18-p. 96 line 20; p. 186 line 22-p. 188 line 28), two chapter headings from its table (Chapters XI and XIII), and the four-word p. 591 repair [we three th]at. The remaining scoped text is attributed to the surviving Caxton copy.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for rightplesauntno4400caxtuoft.
evidence.locator: metadata fields and complete file inventory
evidence.summary: The exact record identifies the 1884-85 source, University of Toronto contribution, public 2006 ingest, scan derivatives, and downloadable files; it contains no rights or license field for the scan or derivatives.

evidence.citation: Early English Text Society, List of Publications 2026, Extra Series 44-45.
evidence.locator: Extra Series 44 and 45
evidence.summary: The list identifies Part I, ES 44, as issued in 1884 with ISBN 9780859917308 and Part II, ES 45, as issued in 1885 with ISBN 9780859917599. These issue years remain distinct from the 1885 and 1886 title-page evidence.

evidence.citation: CERL ISTC ia01434500, Four sons of Aymon [English].
evidence.locator: title, translator, commission, imprint, references, and holdings
evidence.summary: ISTC identifies Caxton's Middle English translation, John Earl of Oxford's commission, Westminster printing about 1490, Manchester's imperfect copy, and a Cambridge fragment; its still-current four-leaf Cambridge statement is retained separately from Cambridge's current two-leaf record.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library, Early Printing Collection, Bookreader 19180 / reference 19180.
evidence.locator: full item metadata
evidence.summary: The exact record identifies the about-1490 Westminster Middle English print, reference 19180, dimensions, provenance, standard catalog numbers, image capture, image rights holder, and CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library IIIF Presentation manifest for reference 19180.
evidence.locator: complete 550-canvas sequence and exact Image API services
evidence.summary: The manifest exposes all 550 ordered canvases of the physical object and exact IIIF image services. Its license field is null, so the item record rather than the manifest supplies the item-level image license.

evidence.citation: Visual inspection of all Manchester reference 19180 IIIF canvases and representative full views.
evidence.locator: canvases 1-550; surviving target text canvases 11-544
evidence.summary: Inspection confirms binding-to-binding digital coverage and maps surviving Caxton text from canvas 11, signature B.iii.a at Richardson p. 24 line 12, through canvas 544 and AMEN. Canvases 77-80 are blank surfaces at a documented internal loss, and the physical witness lacks Caxton's colophon.

evidence.citation: University of Manchester Library, Digital collections help and support.
evidence.locator: About Library Digitised Collections
evidence.summary: The Library says its digitized-collection images and metadata are normally produced by its Heritage Imaging Team and published under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 for download and reuse under that license.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, iDiscover record alma9948278273403606, Inc.3.J.1.1[3522].
evidence.locator: title, uniform title, imprint, commission, fragment note, provenance, and shelfmark
evidence.summary: The current record gives shelfmark Inc.3.J.1.1[3522] and states that two leaves of gathering E now remain, formerly four, with the other two missing since at least 1981. This current item-level evidence is preserved alongside ISTC's still-current four-leaf statement.

evidence.citation: British Library current catalog record alma990001542870109251, C.12.i.7.
evidence.locator: title, 1554 Copland/Petet imprint, extent, holding, and shelfmark
evidence.summary: The exact current record identifies Richardson's supply copy as British Library C.12.i.7, printed by William Copland for Thomas Petet on 6 May 1554, with five preliminary and 174 numbered leaves.

evidence.citation: British Library current catalog record alma990001542610109251, IB.42244.
evidence.locator: title, historical 1480? date, 228-leaf extent, textual opening and ending, and shelfmark
evidence.summary: The current record preserves an older Lyons 1480? description and identifies shelfmark IB.42244. Its repository, format, 228-leaf extent, missing first and last leaves, opening, and explicit identify Richardson's French comparison volume; modern ISTC assigns the edition to the Printer of L'Abusé en court about 1485.
notes: This evidence identifies Richardson's comparison witness only and does not identify Caxton's physical French exemplar.

evidence.citation: Lotte Hellinga, 'From Poggio to Caxton: Early Translations of Some of Poggio's Latin Facetiae,' in Texts in Transit (Brill, 2014), pp. 258-259.
evidence.summary: Hellinga dates Caxton's Four Sons of Aymon about 1490 and identifies two plausible Lyon source classes: Guillaume Le Roy about 1482-85 and the Printer of L'Abusé en court about 1485. She states that the exact choice cannot be made without extensive textual investigation.

evidence.citation: CERL ISTC ia01432800, Les quatre fils Aymon, Guillaume Le Roy edition.
evidence.locator: imprint, date alternatives, holdings, references, and BnF digital copy
evidence.summary: ISTC describes the Lyon Guillaume Le Roy French print as about 1482-85, with CIBN dating about 1485-86, and links BnF RES-Y2-365. It is a candidate source class, not an identified physical Caxton exemplar.

evidence.citation: Gallica IIIF Presentation manifest and item page for BnF RES-Y2-365, Guillaume Le Roy's Les quatre fils Aymon.
evidence.locator: manifest metadata, license, 456 ordered canvases, and exact per-canvas Image API services
evidence.summary: The exact manifest identifies a BnF Réserve des livres rares holding, attributes the object to the BnF, links Gallica's terms, and exposes a complete 456-canvas facsimile with direct IIIF image services. No partner-source or other item-specific exception is stated.
notes: The complete supporting-source bundle retains the original Presentation manifest and every exact successful Image API request in inventory.json.
The French source work is present, but the cataloged English target work is absent.

evidence.citation: CERL ISTC ia01432900, Les quatre fils Aymon, Printer of L'Abusé en court edition.
evidence.locator: imprint, date alternatives, holdings, references, and BnF digital copy
evidence.summary: ISTC describes the Lyon Printer of L'Abusé en court French print as about 1485, with CIBN dating about 1483-85, and lists BnF RES-Y2-364 and BL IB.42244 among its copies. It is a candidate source class; the BL copy is Richardson's comparison witness, not thereby Caxton's exemplar.

evidence.citation: Gallica IIIF Presentation manifest and item page for BnF RES-Y2-364, Printer of L'Abusé en court Les quatre fils Aymon.
evidence.locator: manifest metadata, license, 465 ordered canvases, and exact per-canvas Image API services
evidence.summary: The exact manifest identifies a BnF Réserve des livres rares holding, attributes the object to the BnF, links Gallica's terms, and exposes a complete 465-canvas facsimile with direct IIIF image services. No partner-source or other item-specific exception is stated.
notes: The complete supporting-source bundle retains the original Presentation manifest and every exact successful Image API request in inventory.json.
The French source work is present, but the cataloged English target work is absent; this BnF copy is not BL IB.42244.

evidence.citation: Université de Caen Normandie, Éditions Lyonnaises de Romans, [Renaut de Montauban] Quatre fils Aymon.
evidence.locator: work description and early Lyon editions
evidence.summary: The research edition describes the anonymous prose vulgate as a medieval French adventure romance related to Arsenal 3151, records the two early Lyon incunables and surviving copies, and notes the traditional twenty-eight-chapter organization.

evidence.citation: LICENSE file in the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME repository.
evidence.locator: complete Unlicense text
evidence.summary: The repository notice dedicates software to the public domain under the Unlicense, but does not expressly separate or identify the copyright status of embedded historical texts, third-party transcriptions, or XML editorial markup.

evidence.citation: United States Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright, revised April 2026.
evidence.locator: Complete official four-page PDF, April 2026 revision; duration tables for published works
evidence.summary: The cached exact official circular supplies the current term guidance used for the United States public-domain assessments of the fifteenth- and sixteenth-century prints and Richardson's nineteenth-century editions; as a supporting legal source, it contains none of the cataloged work.

evidence.citation: United States Code, title 17, section 105, Subject matter of copyright: United States Government works.
evidence.locator: Operative rule and statutory exceptions
evidence.summary: Official statutory text states that United States copyright protection is unavailable for United States Government works, subject to the section's specified exceptions.

evidence.citation: Gallica, Conditions d'utilisation.
evidence.locator: preamble, noncommercial and commercial reuse provisions, metadata license, and API statement
evidence.summary: Gallica says noncommercial reuse of BnF public-domain reproductions is free with prescribed BnF/Gallica attribution, metadata is under Licence Ouverte/Etalab, commercial reuse requires a paid license, and its IIIF API is available for image retrieval. The page separately warns that protected and partner-source items can have special regimes.

open_questions.question: Which physical French prose book did Caxton translate, and was it a copy of either early Lyon edition class identified by Hellinga?
next_steps: Undertake or consult a full textual and typographical comparison of Caxton against both Lyon print states and the broader prose tradition.
Seek item-level provenance and copy-specific readings rather than selecting an edition from date and textual similarity alone.
Do not equate Richardson's British Library IB.42244 comparison copy with Caxton's exemplar without direct provenance or textual proof.
notes: The two complete BnF facsimiles are retained as supporting candidate-source objects only; neither is linked as Caxton's identified physical exemplar.

open_questions.question: Can separate physical title leaves or wrappers for both original Richardson issues be inspected to confirm the XML's 1885 and 1886 title-page dates against the EETS issue years 1884 and 1885?
next_steps: Inspect complete original ES 44 and ES 45 issues separately, including wrappers and title leaves.
Retain issue year and title-page carrier date as distinct assertions until both physical carriers have been checked.
notes: The combined scan directly shows a title page dated 1885 but begins its Part II run at page 321 without a separately inspectable Part II title leaf.

open_questions.question: When and under what circumstances were two of Cambridge's formerly four gathering-E leaves lost?
next_steps: Consult Cambridge conservation, accession, and reader-use records before and after 1981.
Notify ISTC of the current two-leaf item record if the discrepancy has not already entered its revision queue.

open_questions.question: How should U-M's current Public Domain / No Copyright-United States label be reconciled with the less specific distribution-restriction warning embedded in the 2006 AHA2639 XML?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections whether the current exact-item statement supersedes, narrows, or merely supplements the embedded availability text.
Until clarified, preserve both assertions and avoid claiming an unrestricted worldwide license for the encoding or markup.

open_questions.question: What exact U-M release, download date, and transformation history produced the pinned local AHA2639 XML blob?
next_steps: Inspect older CME repository history and surviving U-M bulk-download documentation.
Record any intervening normalization, repair, or identifier transformation when documentary evidence is found.

open_questions.question: Can Caxton's translation and first printing be dated more narrowly than about 1490?
next_steps: Review current type, paper, and publishing-chronology evidence behind ISTC's about-1490 date.
Retain Richardson's historical about-1489 dating as evidence rather than silently replacing it.

review_notes: The cataloged work is Caxton's Middle English prose translation/version of the anonymous French prose Les quatre fils Aymon: Caxton's prologue, table, and twenty-eight chapters. Richardson's introduction, plot sketch, apparatus, glossarial index, and name index are editorial carrier matter rather than components of the work.

The lineage has twenty-one separately modeled entities. In addition to the repository, encoding, edition, work, print-state, catalog, and physical-witness layers, Manchester's IIIF presentation and each Gallica presentation are distinct facsimiles; each BnF shelfmark is also distinct from its Lyon edition/state. The official USCO circular is a supporting legal-source catalog entity outside the textual lineage.

Richardson Part I is EETS ES 44 issued 1884 with a preserved title page dated 1885. Part II is ES 45 issued 1885, while the XML's concatenated carrier transcription supplies 1886; the combined scan does not expose a separate Part II title leaf.

Seven exact Copland interventions are modeled as separate passage-scoped relations: four prose supplies, two table-derived chapter headings, and the four-word page-591 repair [we three th]at. The remaining edition-to-Caxton relations expressly exclude those passages.

British Library IB.42244 is modeled only as Richardson's French comparison witness. Its assignment to the Printer of L'Abusé en court edition does not identify it, or that edition class, as Caxton's physical exemplar.

The distinct Manchester IIIF facsimile and cache contain all 550 canvases of physical reference 19180. Surviving target text maps to canvases 11-544; the physical witness begins within Chapter I, has documented internal losses, ends with AMEN, and lacks the colophon.

The complete 456- and 465-canvas Gallica bundles reproduce the exact BnF RES-Y2-365 and RES-Y2-364 copies, which are separately related to the two plausible Lyon edition states. They contain the French source work but not the cataloged English target, so target_work_presence is absent and no work_portion is asserted; candidate-comparison loci belong only in scoped relations and notes.

U-M's current Public Domain / No Copyright-United States presentation, its embedded legacy availability warning, the CME source repository's software-focused Unlicense, historical text rights, and modern image-provider terms are retained as separate claims.

The exact April 2026 USCO Circular 15A is modeled as a target-absent supporting legal source. Its verified local PDF is an intentional hard link to an existing corpus cache copy; 17 U.S.C. 105 separately supports its United States government-work public-domain status.

All source-cache files are gitignored research copies. Local presence and complete source-object coverage do not authorize redistribution; Manchester images remain CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 and Gallica commercial reuse requires its paid-license route.
